name: Design & Analysis of Autonomous Firefighting Robot
shortDescription: An autonomous robot engineered to navigate buildings independently and extinguish fires using sensor-driven navigation and a mechanically robust chassis.
problem: Fire incidents in enclosed structures put first responders at high risk. The team needed a low-cost, autonomous ground robot capable of navigating unpredictable indoor environments and suppressing small fires without human intervention.
objectives: Design a structurally rigid, lightweight chassis capable of carrying a suppression payload.
Integrate flame, gas, and obstacle sensors into the mechanical layout.
Develop a navigation and control strategy for autonomous indoor movement.
Validate structural and thermal performance through simulation before fabrication.
engineeringChallenge: Balancing chassis rigidity against overall robot weight while leaving sufficient volume for the sensor suite, suppression tank, and drivetrain, and ensuring components could tolerate elevated near-fire temperatures.
solution: Modeled the full chassis and drivetrain assembly in CAD, ran structural and thermal simulations to validate material choices, and iterated the sensor-mounting geometry to minimize blind spots while keeping the center of gravity low for stability.
cad: Full 3D assembly modeled with exploded views for the chassis, drivetrain, and sensor mounts; GD&T applied to critical mating features.
manufacturing: Prototype frame fabricated using laser-cut sheet metal panels and 3D-printed sensor brackets, assembled and tested on a benchtop rig.
results: Delivered a working prototype capable of autonomous indoor navigation.
Validated chassis structural integrity via simulation prior to fabrication, avoiding a costly redesign cycle.
Strengthened team understanding of automation, robotics integration, and fire-safety system design.

name: JARVIS Voice-Controlled Machine Assistant
shortDescription: A voice-activated virtual assistant coupled to specific shop equipment (drilling, cutting) that launches machine operations through spoken commands.
problem: Operators frequently needed to run repetitive machine sequences (drilling, cutting) while their hands were occupied, creating workflow friction and safety risk at the point of operation.
objectives: Build a voice-recognition front end that maps spoken keywords to specific machine routines.
Write the control logic and query set required to run the target equipment reliably.
Ensure the system fails safely if a command is misheard or ambiguous.
engineeringChallenge: Reliably mapping natural speech input to discrete machine-control commands while preventing accidental triggering of hazardous equipment.
solution: Built a Python-based voice assistant that listens for a defined vocabulary of trigger phrases, matches them against a query set, and executes the corresponding machine routine, with explicit fallback behavior for unrecognized input.
manufacturing: Tested against a benchtop drilling/cutting setup to validate command-to-action reliability.
results: Successfully triggered target machine routines from voice commands in test conditions.
Demonstrated a low-cost path to hands-free machine control for repetitive shop tasks.
